chunk_summary: Donor human milk in India is prioritized for preterm, low birth weight, and sick neonates in NICU/SNCU settings, with strict screening, pasteurization, and operational protocols to ensure safety and nutritional adequacy.
learning_objectives: Understand the indications for donor human milk in preterm and low birth weight infants in India.
Learn the screening and pasteurization protocols for donor human milk to ensure safety.
Explore operational models like CLMCs and LMUs for donor milk access in neonatal care.
Recognize the nutritional and immunological benefits and limitations of pasteurized donor milk.
key_facts: Donor milk is prioritized for very preterm or very low birth weight infants and sick neonates in NICU/SNCU settings.
Screening includes HIV 1/2, hepatitis B, syphilis (VDRL), and hepatitis C as per local protocols.
Holder pasteurization is performed at 62.5°C for 30 minutes with rapid cooling to reduce infection risks.
Pasteurization inactivates CMV and HIV while retaining most nutritional value, though some immune factors are reduced.
Comprehensive Lactation Management Centres (CLMCs) and Lactation Management Units (LMUs) operationalize donor milk access in higher-level facilities.
reference_quote: National lactation management guidance positions pasteurized donor human milk as a bridge for admitted newborns when mother’s own milk is temporarily unavailable or insufficient.
document_key_topics: Donor human milk indications for preterm and low birth weight infants
Screening and pasteurization protocols for donor milk safety
Operational models for donor milk access in India
